title: CLI Error Handling and Exit Codes
content:
Good error handling is what separates a polished CLI from a frustrating one. Users need clear, actionable error messages -- not stack traces or cryptic codes.

Return anyhow::Result from main() for automatic error formatting. Use .with_context() to add information about what you were trying to do when the error occurred. Use std::process::exit() when you need specific exit codes.

Convention: exit code 0 means success, 1 means general error, 2 means usage error (bad arguments). Match the conventions of similar tools.
keyPoints: Return Result from main() for automatic error display
Use eprintln! for error output (goes to stderr, not stdout)
with_context() adds 'what were you doing' information to errors
bail!() is a shortcut for return Err(anyhow!(...))
Use {:#} format specifier to print the full error chain
Exit code 0 = success, 1 = error, 2 = usage error
